transcript:
Here's what you're seeing: rows of sharp white text sliding in from the right, like scrolling readout data — and as each phrase drifts left, it doesn't just fade, it comes apart into grain, until the whole row is just soft atmospheric dust.

The trick is in "render memory" — it's not one fade, it's four distinct phases keyed to age. Under two seconds old, text renders as crisp glowing words. By five seconds it's decomposed into individual vertical strokes — literally, the code breaks each letter into its own "letter offset" and "stroke height." Past ten seconds, it stops drawing strokes entirely and starts scattering soft particles instead — look for "particle count" in that final phase, where meaning becomes literal noise. The words themselves, if you could still read them by then, say things like "already fading" and "precision lost" — the content is narrating its own decay in real time.

So the piece isn't showing you an image dissolving. It's showing you the actual moment legible language becomes texture — and choosing, deliberately, words about forgetting to be the thing that gets forgotten.
